source: Security Enhancements - Introduction to PAM and basic usage: Key PAM modules and the password-complexity/lockout/history cases

options: Prompts for a password and checks it against /etc/shadow
questions.why: pam_unix is the standard UNIX password authentication module.

options: Always returns failure -- used as a catch-all reject at the end of a chain
questions.why: pam_deny always says 'no'; unmatched requests fall through to it and are rejected.

questions.q: pam_pwquality.so checks new-password complexity. What does dcredit=-1 require?
options: At least one digit in the new password (a negative credit sets a minimum of that class)
questions.why: N<0 means 'at least |N| of this class required'; N>0 would be a maximum credit.

questions.q: Which pam_pwquality parameter makes the complexity rules also apply to root?
questions.why: Without enforce_for_root the checks are advisory for root.

questions.q: What does pam_faillock.so do, and which parameters control it?
options: Locks an account after too many failed attempts: deny=N, unlock_time=secs, fail_interval=secs, even_deny_root
questions.why: faillock counts failures in fail_interval and locks for unlock_time after deny attempts.

questions.q: View the current per-user faillock records.
questions.why: faillock shows failed-login records; faillock --reset clears them (data in /var/run/faillock/).

questions.q: pam_pwhistory.so with remember=3 does what, and where are the old hashes stored?
options: Forbids reusing the last 3 passwords; old hashes are recorded in /etc/security/opasswd
Locks the account after 3 tries; stored in /etc/shadow
questions.why: pwhistory blocks reuse of the last N passwords, tracked in /etc/security/opasswd.

questions.q: What do pam_wheel.so, pam_nologin.so, and pam_time.so do?
options: wheel: permit root access only to the wheel group; nologin: block non-root logins; time: time-based access control
questions.why: Three targeted modules: group-gating su/root, blocking non-root login, and time-of-day control.

questions.q: How do you check which facilities (module types) a given module supports?
options: Read its man page, e.g. man 8 pam_unix (the MODULE TYPES PROVIDED section)
questions.why: man 8 <module> lists the module types it provides.
